Mx+(aq)/M(s)Al3+/AlMg2+/MgLi+/LiZn2+/Zn
EMx+/M∘ (V)−1.66−2.36−3.05−0.76

Which metal anode will produce the maximum standard electrical work per mole of the solid metal consumed?|
questions.questionHtml:
Consider a galvanic cell constructed by coupling a standard Cu2+(aq)/Cu(s) cathode (E∘=+0.34 V) with different metal anodes, M(s)∣Mx+(aq). The standard reduction potentials for various anode half-cells are given below:

Mx+(aq)/M(s)Al3+/AlMg2+/MgLi+/LiZn2+/Zn
EMx+/M∘ (V)−1.66−2.36−3.05−0.76

Which metal anode will produce the maximum standard electrical work per mole of the solid metal consumed?|
questions.solutionText: Solution The standard electrical work produced by a galvanic cell is given by W m a x = − Δ G ∘ = n F E c e l l ∘ . For 1 mole of metal anode M consumed, the number of moles of electrons transferred is n = x , where x is the valency of the metal ion M x + . The standard cell potential is E c e l l ∘ = E c a t h o d e ∘ − E a n o d e ∘ = 0.34 − E M x + / M ∘ . Calculating the work per mole of metal consumed (in terms of F , W / F = n × E c e l l ∘ ) for each option: For Al ( n = 3 ): E c e l l ∘ = 0.34 − ( − 1.66 ) = 2.00 V ⇒ W / F = 3 × 2.00 = 6.00 V For Mg ( n = 2 ): E c e l l ∘ = 0.34 − ( − 2.36 ) = 2.70 V ⇒ W / F = 2 × 2.70 = 5.40 V For Li ( n = 1 ): E c e l l ∘ = 0.34 − ( − 3.05 ) = 3.39 V ⇒ W / F = 1 × 3.39 = 3.39 V For Zn ( n = 2 ): E c e l l ∘ = 0.34 − ( − 0.76 ) = 1.10 V ⇒ W / F = 2 × 1.10 = 2.20 V The maximum standard electrical work per mole of solid metal consumed is produced by Al . Answer: Al

For 1 mole of metal anode M consumed, the number of moles of electrons transferred is n = x , where x is the valency of the metal ion M x + . The standard cell potential is E c e l l ∘ = E c a t h o d e ∘ − E a n o d e ∘ = 0.34 − E M x + / M ∘ . Calculating the work per mole of metal consumed (in terms of F , W / F = n × E c e l l ∘ ) for each option: For Al ( n = 3 ): E c e l l ∘ = 0.34 − ( − 1.66 ) = 2.00 V ⇒ W / F = 3 × 2.00 = 6.00 V For Mg ( n = 2 ): E c e l l ∘ = 0.34 − ( − 2.36 ) = 2.70 V ⇒ W / F = 2 × 2.70 = 5.40 V For Li ( n = 1 ): E c e l l ∘ = 0.34 − ( − 3.05 ) = 3.39 V ⇒ W / F = 1 × 3.39 = 3.39 V For Zn ( n = 2 ): E c e l l ∘ = 0.34 − ( − 0.76 ) = 1.10 V ⇒ W / F = 2 × 1.10 = 2.20 V The maximum standard electrical work per mole of solid metal consumed is produced by Al . Answer: Al
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
The standard electrical work produced by a galvanic cell is given by Wmax=−ΔG∘=nFEcell∘.

For 1 mole of metal anode M consumed, the number of moles of electrons transferred is n=x, where x is the valency of the metal ion Mx+.

The standard cell potential is Ecell∘=Ecathode∘−Eanode∘=0.34−EMx+/M∘.

Calculating the work per mole of metal consumed (in terms of F, W/F=n×Ecell∘) for each option:

For Al (n=3): Ecell∘=0.34−(−1.66)=2.00 V⇒W/F=3×2.00=6.00 V

For Mg (n=2): Ecell∘=0.34−(−2.36)=2.70 V⇒W/F=2×2.70=5.40 V

For Li (n=1): Ecell∘=0.34−(−3.05)=3.39 V⇒W/F=1×3.39=3.39 V

For Zn (n=2): Ecell∘=0.34−(−0.76)=1.10 V⇒W/F=2×1.10=2.20 V

The maximum standard electrical work per mole of solid metal consumed is produced by Al.

Answer: Al

questions.questionHtml:
Consider the following statements regarding smog:
(A) Classical smog is reducing in nature.
(B) Photochemical smog contains formaldehyde and PAN.
(C) Classical smog requires warm, dry climates.
(D) Photochemical smog involves primarily sulfur dioxide.

Choose the correct statements:
questions.solutionText: Solution Classical smog occurs in cool, humid climates and is a mixture of smoke, fog, and sulphur dioxide. Chemically, it is a reducing mixture and is known as reducing smog. Thus, statement (A) is correct and statement (C) is incorrect. Photochemical smog occurs in warm, dry, and sunny climates. It is formed by the action of sunlight on unsaturated hydrocarbons and nitrogen oxides. Its main components include ozone, formaldehyde, acrolein, and peroxyacetyl nitrate (PAN). Thus, statement (B) is correct and statement (D) is incorrect. Therefore, only statements (A) and (B) are correct. Answer: (A) and (B) only
questions.solution: Solution Classical smog occurs in cool, humid climates and is a mixture of smoke, fog, and sulphur dioxide. Chemically, it is a reducing mixture and is known as reducing smog. Thus, statement (A) is correct and statement (C) is incorrect. Photochemical smog occurs in warm, dry, and sunny climates. It is formed by the action of sunlight on unsaturated hydrocarbons and nitrogen oxides. Its main components include ozone, formaldehyde, acrolein, and peroxyacetyl nitrate (PAN). Thus, statement (B) is correct and statement (D) is incorrect. Therefore, only statements (A) and (B) are correct. Answer: (A) and (B) only
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Classical smog occurs in cool, humid climates and is a mixture of smoke, fog, and sulphur dioxide. Chemically, it is a reducing mixture and is known as reducing smog. Thus, statement (A) is correct and statement (C) is incorrect.

Photochemical smog occurs in warm, dry, and sunny climates. It is formed by the action of sunlight on unsaturated hydrocarbons and nitrogen oxides. Its main components include ozone, formaldehyde, acrolein, and peroxyacetyl nitrate (PAN). Thus, statement (B) is correct and statement (D) is incorrect.

Therefore, only statements (A) and (B) are correct.

Answer: (A) and (B) only

questions.questionHtml: The correct IUPAC name for the compound
questions.solutionText: Solution The principal functional group is the − O H group, so the suffix is ' − o l '. The longest carbon chain containing the − O H group and the multiple bonds has 6 carbons, so the parent chain is 'hex'. Numbering the chain to give the lowest possible locant to the principal functional group ( − O H ): Numbering from left to right gives the − O H group position 3. Numbering from right to left gives the − O H group position 4. Thus, the correct numbering is from left to right. With this numbering, the substituents and multiple bonds are: Chloro group at C2 → 2-chloro Double bond at C1 → 1-en Triple bond at C5 → 5-yn The multiple bonds are cited in alphabetical order ('en' before 'yn'). The 'e' from 'ene' is dropped as it is followed by 'y', and the 'e' from 'yne' is dropped as it is followed by 'o'. Combining these parts, the IUPAC name is 2-chlorohex-1-en-5-yn-3-ol. Answer: 2-chlorohex-1-en-5-yn-3-ol

Numbering from right to left gives the − O H group position 4. Thus, the correct numbering is from left to right. With this numbering, the substituents and multiple bonds are: Chloro group at C2 → 2-chloro Double bond at C1 → 1-en Triple bond at C5 → 5-yn The multiple bonds are cited in alphabetical order ('en' before 'yn'). The 'e' from 'ene' is dropped as it is followed by 'y', and the 'e' from 'yne' is dropped as it is followed by 'o'. Combining these parts, the IUPAC name is 2-chlorohex-1-en-5-yn-3-ol. Answer: 2-chlorohex-1-en-5-yn-3-ol
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
The principal functional group is the −OH group, so the suffix is '−ol'.

The longest carbon chain containing the −OH group and the multiple bonds has 6 carbons, so the parent chain is 'hex'.

Numbering the chain to give the lowest possible locant to the principal functional group (−OH):
Numbering from left to right gives the −OH group position 3.
Numbering from right to left gives the −OH group position 4.
Thus, the correct numbering is from left to right.

With this numbering, the substituents and multiple bonds are:
Chloro group at C2 → 2-chloro
Double bond at C1 → 1-en
Triple bond at C5 → 5-yn

The multiple bonds are cited in alphabetical order ('en' before 'yn'). The 'e' from 'ene' is dropped as it is followed by 'y', and the 'e' from 'yne' is dropped as it is followed by 'o'.

Combining these parts, the IUPAC name is 2-chlorohex-1-en-5-yn-3-ol.

Answer: 2-chlorohex-1-en-5-yn-3-ol

questions.text: A metallic solid forms a body-centered cubic lattice having an atomic radius of 100 3 pm . If its density is 10 × 10 3 kg m − 3 , what is the molar mass of this solid? [Avogadro constant ≈ 6 × 10 23 mol − 1 ]
questions.questionText: A metallic solid forms a body-centered cubic lattice having an atomic radius of 100 3 pm . If its density is 10 × 10 3 kg m − 3 , what is the molar mass of this solid? [Avogadro constant ≈ 6 × 10 23 mol − 1 ]
questions.question: A metallic solid forms a body-centered cubic lattice having an atomic radius of 100 3 pm . If its density is 10 × 10 3 kg m − 3 , what is the molar mass of this solid? [Avogadro constant ≈ 6 × 10 23 mol − 1 ]
questions.prompt: A metallic solid forms a body-centered cubic lattice having an atomic radius of 100 3 pm . If its density is 10 × 10 3 kg m − 3 , what is the molar mass of this solid? [Avogadro constant ≈ 6 × 10 23 mol − 1 ]
questions.html:
A metallic solid forms a body-centered cubic lattice having an atomic radius of 1003pm. If its density is 10×103 kg m−3, what is the molar mass of this solid?
[Avogadro constant ≈6×1023 mol−1 ]
questions.questionHtml:
A metallic solid forms a body-centered cubic lattice having an atomic radius of 1003pm. If its density is 10×103 kg m−3, what is the molar mass of this solid?
[Avogadro constant ≈6×1023 mol−1 ]
questions.solutionText: Solution For a body-centered cubic (bcc) lattice, the relation between the edge length a and the atomic radius r is given by: 3 a = 4 r Substituting the given atomic radius r = 100 3 pm : a = 4 × 100 3 3 = 400 pm = 4 × 10 − 10 m The volume of the unit cell V is: V = a 3 = ( 4 × 10 − 10 m ) 3 = 64 × 10 − 30 m 3 For a bcc lattice, the number of atoms per unit cell is Z = 2 . The density d of the solid is given by the formula: d = Z × M N A × a 3 Rearranging to solve for the molar mass M : M = d × N A × a 3 Z Substituting the given values ( d = 10 × 10 3 kg m − 3 , N A = 6 × 10 23 mol − 1 ): M = 10 × 10 3 × 6 × 10 23 × 64 × 10 − 30 2 M = 384 × 10 − 3 2 M = 0.192 kg mol − 1 Answer: 0.192 kg mol − 1
questions.solution: Solution For a body-centered cubic (bcc) lattice, the relation between the edge length a and the atomic radius r is given by: 3 a = 4 r Substituting the given atomic radius r = 100 3 pm : a = 4 × 100 3 3 = 400 pm = 4 × 10 − 10 m The volume of the unit cell V is: V = a 3 = ( 4 × 10 − 10 m ) 3 = 64 × 10 − 30 m 3 For a bcc lattice, the number of atoms per unit cell is Z = 2 . The density d of the solid is given by the formula: d = Z × M N A × a 3 Rearranging to solve for the molar mass M : M = d × N A × a 3 Z Substituting the given values ( d = 10 × 10 3 kg m − 3 , N A = 6 × 10 23 mol − 1 ): M = 10 × 10 3 × 6 × 10 23 × 64 × 10 − 30 2 M = 384 × 10 − 3 2 M = 0.192 kg mol − 1 Answer: 0.192 kg mol − 1
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
For a body-centered cubic (bcc) lattice, the relation between the edge length a and the atomic radius r is given by:

3a=4r

Substituting the given atomic radius r=1003 pm:

a=4×10033=400 pm=4×10−10 m

The volume of the unit cell V is:

V=a3=(4×10−10 m)3=64×10−30 m3

For a bcc lattice, the number of atoms per unit cell is Z=2.

The density d of the solid is given by the formula:

d=Z×MNA×a3

Rearranging to solve for the molar mass M:

M=d×NA×a3Z

Substituting the given values (d=10×103 kg m−3, NA=6×1023 mol−1):

M=10×103×6×1023×64×10−302

M=384×10−32

M=0.192 kg mol−1

Answer: 0.192 kg mol−1

questions.text: 1 mole of N 2 gas and w grams of H 2 gas exert a total pressure of 200 Pa in a container of volume 5 m 3 at 200 K . Given R is the gas constant in J K − 1 mol − 1 , w is:
questions.questionText: 1 mole of N 2 gas and w grams of H 2 gas exert a total pressure of 200 Pa in a container of volume 5 m 3 at 200 K . Given R is the gas constant in J K − 1 mol − 1 , w is:
questions.question: 1 mole of N 2 gas and w grams of H 2 gas exert a total pressure of 200 Pa in a container of volume 5 m 3 at 200 K . Given R is the gas constant in J K − 1 mol − 1 , w is:
questions.prompt: 1 mole of N 2 gas and w grams of H 2 gas exert a total pressure of 200 Pa in a container of volume 5 m 3 at 200 K . Given R is the gas constant in J K − 1 mol − 1 , w is:
questions.html: 1 mole of N2 gas and w grams of H2 gas exert a total pressure of 200 Pa in a container of volume 5 m3 at 200 K . Given R is the gas constant in JK−1 mol−1,w is:
questions.questionHtml: 1 mole of N2 gas and w grams of H2 gas exert a total pressure of 200 Pa in a container of volume 5 m3 at 200 K . Given R is the gas constant in JK−1 mol−1,w is:
questions.solutionText: Solution Using the ideal gas equation P V = n t o t a l R T Substituting the given values: 200 × 5 = n t o t a l × R × 200 n t o t a l = 1000 200 R = 5 R The total number of moles is the sum of moles of N 2 and H 2 . n t o t a l = n N 2 + n H 2 Given n N 2 = 1 and n H 2 = w 2 (since the molar mass of H 2 is 2 g mol − 1 ) 5 R = 1 + w 2 w 2 = 5 R − 1 = 5 − R R w = 10 − 2 R R Answer: 10 − 2 R R
questions.solution: Solution Using the ideal gas equation P V = n t o t a l R T Substituting the given values: 200 × 5 = n t o t a l × R × 200 n t o t a l = 1000 200 R = 5 R The total number of moles is the sum of moles of N 2 and H 2 . n t o t a l = n N 2 + n H 2 Given n N 2 = 1 and n H 2 = w 2 (since the molar mass of H 2 is 2 g mol − 1 ) 5 R = 1 + w 2 w 2 = 5 R − 1 = 5 − R R w = 10 − 2 R R Answer: 10 − 2 R R
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Using the ideal gas equation PV=ntotalRT

Substituting the given values:

200×5=ntotal×R×200

ntotal=1000200R=5R

The total number of moles is the sum of moles of N2 and H2.

ntotal=nN2+nH2

Given nN2=1 and nH2=w2 (since the molar mass of H2 is 2 g mol−1)

5R=1+w2

w2=5R−1=5−RR

w=10−2RR

Answer: 10−2RR

(D) Silica gel strongly adsorbs polar organic compounds.
(E) The maximum possible Rf value is one.

Choose the correct option:
questions.questionHtml:
Read the following statements regarding Thin Layer Chromatography (TLC):
(A) Strong adsorption gives a higher Rf value.
(B) Rf depends on the chosen mobile phase.
(C) Rf is solvent distance over solute distance.
(D) Silica gel strongly adsorbs polar organic compounds.
(E) The maximum possible Rf value is one.

Choose the correct option:
questions.solutionText: Solution Statement (A) is incorrect because strong adsorption means the compound is held tightly by the stationary phase, resulting in a shorter distance travelled and a lower R f value. Statement (B) is correct because the R f value depends on the nature of the mobile phase (solvent) and the stationary phase. Statement (C) is incorrect because the retardation factor R f is defined as the ratio of the distance travelled by the solute to the distance travelled by the solvent front. Statement (D) is correct because silica gel is a polar stationary phase and strongly adsorbs polar organic compounds through dipole-dipole interactions or hydrogen bonding. Statement (E) is correct because the solute cannot travel further than the solvent front, making the maximum possible R f value equal to 1 . Therefore, statements (B), (D), and (E) are correct. Answer: (B), (D) and (E) only

(B) Maximum coordination number of Beryllium is six.
(C) Magnesium imparts crimson red color to flame.
(D) Beryllium ion has the highest hydration enthalpy.
(E) BeO and Be(OH)2 are amphoteric in nature.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
questions.questionHtml:
Identify the correct statements regarding alkaline earth metals:
(A) BeCl2 is highly soluble in organic solvents.
(B) Maximum coordination number of Beryllium is six.
(C) Magnesium imparts crimson red color to flame.
(D) Beryllium ion has the highest hydration enthalpy.
(E) BeO and Be(OH)2 are amphoteric in nature.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
questions.solutionText: Solution Statement (A) is correct because BeCl 2 is a predominantly covalent compound due to the high polarizing power of the small Be 2 + ion, making it highly soluble in organic solvents. Statement (B) is incorrect because Beryllium has only four orbitals in its valence shell ( 2 s and 2 p ) and lacks d -orbitals. Therefore, its maximum coordination number is restricted to four. Statement (C) is incorrect because Magnesium (along with Beryllium) does not impart any color to the flame. The valence electrons are strongly bound and are not excited by the energy of a Bunsen flame. Statement (D) is correct because hydration enthalpy is inversely proportional to ionic size. Since Be 2 + is the smallest ion among the alkaline earth metals, it has the highest hydration enthalpy. Statement (E) is correct because BeO and Be ( OH ) 2 react with both acids and bases, exhibiting amphoteric nature, unlike the oxides and hydroxides of other alkaline earth metals which are basic. Thus, the correct statements are (A), (D), and (E). Answer: (A), (D) and (E) only

questions.text: A solid metallic sphere of constant heat capacity C at an initial temperature T 1 is completely immersed in a massive liquid bath maintained at a constant temperature T 2 . questions.html: A solid metallic sphere of constant heat capacity C at an initial temperature T1 is completely immersed in a massive liquid bath maintained at a constant temperature T2. If the process occurs at constant pressure, the total entropy change of the universe ( ΔSuniverse ) upon reaching thermal equilibrium is:
questions.questionHtml: A solid metallic sphere of constant heat capacity C at an initial temperature T1 is completely immersed in a massive liquid bath maintained at a constant temperature T2. If the process occurs at constant pressure, the total entropy change of the universe ( ΔSuniverse ) upon reaching thermal equilibrium is:
questions.solutionText: Solution The total entropy change of the universe is the sum of the entropy change of the sphere and the entropy change of the liquid bath. The entropy change of the sphere as its temperature changes from T 1 to T 2 is given by: Δ S sphere = ∫ T 1 T 2 C T d T = C ln ⁡ ( T 2 T 1 ) The heat exchanged by the sphere is Q = C ( T 2 − T 1 ) . The heat absorbed by the liquid bath is the negative of the heat exchanged by the sphere, so Q bath = − C ( T 2 − T 1 ) = C ( T 1 − T 2 ) . Since the liquid bath is massive, its temperature remains constant at T 2 . The entropy change of the bath is: Δ S bath = Q bath T 2 = C ( T 1 − T 2 ) T 2 = C ( T 1 T 2 − 1 ) The total entropy change of the universe is: Δ S universe = Δ S sphere + Δ S bath = C [ ln ⁡ ( T 2 T 1 ) + T 1 T 2 − 1 ] Answer: C [ ln ⁡ ( T 2 T 1 ) + T 1 T 2 − 1 ]
questions.solution: Solution The total entropy change of the universe is the sum of the entropy change of the sphere and the entropy change of the liquid bath. The entropy change of the sphere as its temperature changes from T 1 to T 2 is given by: Δ S sphere = ∫ T 1 T 2 C T d T = C ln ⁡ ( T 2 T 1 ) The heat exchanged by the sphere is Q = C ( T 2 − T 1 ) . The heat absorbed by the liquid bath is the negative of the heat exchanged by the sphere, so Q bath = − C ( T 2 − T 1 ) = C ( T 1 − T 2 ) . Since the liquid bath is massive, its temperature remains constant at T 2 . The entropy change of the bath is: Δ S bath = Q bath T 2 = C ( T 1 − T 2 ) T 2 = C ( T 1 T 2 − 1 ) The total entropy change of the universe is: Δ S universe = Δ S sphere + Δ S bath = C [ ln ⁡ ( T 2 T 1 ) + T 1 T 2 − 1 ] Answer: C [ ln ⁡ ( T 2 T 1 ) + T 1 T 2 − 1 ]
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
The total entropy change of the universe is the sum of the entropy change of the sphere and the entropy change of the liquid bath.

The entropy change of the sphere as its temperature changes from T1 to T2 is given by:

ΔSsphere=∫T1T2CTdT=Cln⁡(T2T1)

The heat exchanged by the sphere is Q=C(T2−T1).

The heat absorbed by the liquid bath is the negative of the heat exchanged by the sphere, so Qbath=−C(T2−T1)=C(T1−T2).

Since the liquid bath is massive, its temperature remains constant at T2. The entropy change of the bath is:

ΔSbath=QbathT2=C(T1−T2)T2=C(T1T2−1)

The total entropy change of the universe is:

ΔSuniverse=ΔSsphere+ΔSbath=C[ln⁡(T2T1)+T1T2−1]

Answer: C[ln⁡(T2T1)+T1T2−1]

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Consider the reaction of heated in the presence of polyphosphoric acid (PPA). The major aromatic organic product formed is:
questions.questionText: Consider the reaction of heated in the presence of polyphosphoric acid (PPA). The major aromatic organic product formed is:
questions.question: Consider the reaction of heated in the presence of polyphosphoric acid (PPA). The major aromatic organic product formed is:
questions.prompt: Consider the reaction of heated in the presence of polyphosphoric acid (PPA). The major aromatic organic product formed is:
questions.html: Consider the reaction of heated in the presence of polyphosphoric acid (PPA). The major aromatic organic product formed is:
questions.questionHtml: Consider the reaction of heated in the presence of polyphosphoric acid (PPA). The major aromatic organic product formed is:
questions.solutionText: Solution The given reactant is 4-(3-methoxyphenyl)butanoic acid. In this molecule, the methoxy group ( − O C H 3 ) is located at the meta position relative to the butanoic acid side chain. Heating this compound with polyphosphoric acid (PPA) initiates an intramolecular Friedel-Crafts acylation. The PPA helps convert the carboxylic acid into a reactive acylium ion electrophile. The regioselectivity of the ring closure is determined by the directing effects of the substituents on the benzene ring. The methoxy group is a strongly activating, ortho/para-directing group. The electrophilic acylium ion must attack an ortho position relative to the alkyl side chain to form the stable 6-membered tetralone ring. This leaves two possible sites for attack: the position between the alkyl chain and the methoxy group (C2, which is ortho to the methoxy group) or the position para to the methoxy group (C6). Attack at the C2 position is highly sterically hindered due to its location between two substituents. Therefore, cyclization predominantly occurs at the less hindered C6 position, which is para to the activating methoxy group. Cyclization at the para position yields 6-methoxy-1-tetralone. In the structure of 6-methoxy-1-tetralone, the methoxy group is located para to the newly formed ketone carbonyl group's attachment point on the aromatic ring. Comparing the given options, structure (1) correctly shows the methoxy group at the para position relative to the carbonyl group. Structure (2) is 8-methoxytetralone, structure (3) is 7-methoxytetralone, and structure (4) is 5-methoxytetralone.

questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
The given reactant is 4-(3-methoxyphenyl)butanoic acid. In this molecule, the methoxy group (−OCH3) is located at the meta position relative to the butanoic acid side chain.

Heating this compound with polyphosphoric acid (PPA) initiates an intramolecular Friedel-Crafts acylation. The PPA helps convert the carboxylic acid into a reactive acylium ion electrophile.

The regioselectivity of the ring closure is determined by the directing effects of the substituents on the benzene ring. The methoxy group is a strongly activating, ortho/para-directing group. The electrophilic acylium ion must attack an ortho position relative to the alkyl side chain to form the stable 6-membered tetralone ring. This leaves two possible sites for attack: the position between the alkyl chain and the methoxy group (C2, which is ortho to the methoxy group) or the position para to the methoxy group (C6).

Attack at the C2 position is highly sterically hindered due to its location between two substituents. Therefore, cyclization predominantly occurs at the less hindered C6 position, which is para to the activating methoxy group.

Cyclization at the para position yields 6-methoxy-1-tetralone. In the structure of 6-methoxy-1-tetralone, the methoxy group is located para to the newly formed ketone carbonyl group's attachment point on the aromatic ring.

Comparing the given options, structure (1) correctly shows the methoxy group at the para position relative to the carbonyl group. Structure (2) is 8-methoxytetralone, structure (3) is 7-methoxytetralone, and structure (4) is 5-methoxytetralone.

questions.question: Arrange the following monomers in order of decreasing ability to undergo cationic polymerisation. I. CH 2 = CH − C 6 H 4 ( NO 2 ) II. CH 2 = CH − C 6 H 4 ( CH 3 ) III. CH 2 = CH − C 6 H 4 ( OCH 3 )
questions.prompt: Arrange the following monomers in order of decreasing ability to undergo cationic polymerisation. I. CH 2 = CH − C 6 H 4 ( NO 2 ) II. CH 2 = CH − C 6 H 4 ( CH 3 ) III. CH 2 = CH − C 6 H 4 ( OCH 3 )
questions.html:
Arrange the following monomers in order of decreasing ability to undergo cationic polymerisation.
I. CH2=CH−C6H4(NO2)
II. CH2=CH−C6H4(CH3)
III. CH2=CH−C6H4(OCH3)
questions.questionHtml:
Arrange the following monomers in order of decreasing ability to undergo cationic polymerisation.
I. CH2=CH−C6H4(NO2)
II. CH2=CH−C6H4(CH3)
III. CH2=CH−C6H4(OCH3)
questions.solutionText: Solution Cationic polymerisation proceeds via the formation of a carbocation intermediate. The reactivity of the monomer is directly proportional to the stability of the intermediate carbocation formed. The intermediate carbocation is CH 3 − CH + − C 6 H 4 ( G ) . Electron-donating groups stabilize the carbocation through + M or + I effects, while electron-withdrawing groups destabilize it through − M or − I effects. In monomer III, the − OCH 3 group is a strong electron-donating group ( + M effect), which highly stabilizes the carbocation. In monomer II, the − CH 3 group is an electron-donating group ( + I and hyperconjugation), which stabilizes the carbocation but less than − OCH 3 . In monomer I, the − NO 2 group is a strong electron-withdrawing group ( − M and − I effects), which destabilizes the carbocation. Thus, the stability of the intermediate carbocations and the reactivity towards cationic polymerisation follows the order III > II > I. Answer: III > II > I

In monomer II, the − CH 3 group is an electron-donating group ( + I and hyperconjugation), which stabilizes the carbocation but less than − OCH 3 . In monomer I, the − NO 2 group is a strong electron-withdrawing group ( − M and − I effects), which destabilizes the carbocation. Thus, the stability of the intermediate carbocations and the reactivity towards cationic polymerisation follows the order III > II > I. Answer: III > II > I

Solution
Cationic polymerisation proceeds via the formation of a carbocation intermediate. The reactivity of the monomer is directly proportional to the stability of the intermediate carbocation formed.

The intermediate carbocation is CH3−CH+−C6H4(G).

Electron-donating groups stabilize the carbocation through +M or +I effects, while electron-withdrawing groups destabilize it through −M or −I effects.

In monomer III, the −OCH3 group is a strong electron-donating group (+M effect), which highly stabilizes the carbocation.

In monomer II, the −CH3 group is an electron-donating group (+I and hyperconjugation), which stabilizes the carbocation but less than −OCH3.

In monomer I, the −NO2 group is a strong electron-withdrawing group (−M and −I effects), which destabilizes the carbocation.

Thus, the stability of the intermediate carbocations and the reactivity towards cationic polymerisation follows the order III > II > I.

Answer: III > II > I

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which of the following combination of statements is correct regarding the quantum mechanical model of atoms and the properties of orbitals? (A) The number of radial nodes present in a 3d orbital is strictly greater than the number of angular nodes in a 2 p orbital. (B) In a single-electron system such as He + , the energy of an electron in a 3p orbital is greater than that in a 3s orbital. (C) The probability density, | ψ | 2 , evaluated exactly at the nucleus is zero for a 2 p orbital but non-zero for a 2s orbital. (D) The magnitude of the orbital angular momentum for an electron occupying any dorbital is exactly 6 h 2 π .

questions.questionHtml:
Which of the following combination of statements is correct regarding the quantum mechanical model of atoms and the properties of orbitals?
(A) The number of radial nodes present in a 3d orbital is strictly greater than the number of angular nodes in a 2p orbital.
(B) In a single-electron system such as He+, the energy of an electron in a 3p orbital is greater than that in a 3s orbital.
(C) The probability density, |ψ|2, evaluated exactly at the nucleus is zero for a 2p orbital but non-zero for a 2s orbital.
(D) The magnitude of the orbital angular momentum for an electron occupying any dorbital is exactly 6h2π.
questions.solutionText: Solution Statement (A): For a 3d orbital, n = 3 and l = 2 . The number of radial nodes is given by n − l − 1 = 3 − 2 − 1 = 0 . For a 2p orbital, n = 2 and l = 1 . The number of angular nodes is given by l = 1 . Since 0 is not greater than 1 , statement (A) is incorrect. Statement (B): For a single-electron system like He + , the energy of an electron depends only on the principal quantum number n . Thus, the 3s, 3p, and 3d orbitals are degenerate, meaning their energies are equal. Statement (B) is incorrect. Statement (C): The probability density | ψ | 2 at the nucleus ( r = 0 ) is non-zero only for s orbitals ( l = 0 ). For p orbitals ( l = 1 ), the wave function is zero at the nucleus, so | ψ | 2 = 0 . Statement (C) is correct. Statement (D): The orbital angular momentum is given by l ( l + 1 ) h 2 π . For a d orbital, l = 2 , so the angular momentum is 2 ( 2 + 1 ) h 2 π = 6 h 2 π . Statement (D) is correct. Therefore, statements (C) and (D) are correct. Answer: (C), (D)

Statement (B): For a single-electron system like He + , the energy of an electron depends only on the principal quantum number n . Thus, the 3s, 3p, and 3d orbitals are degenerate, meaning their energies are equal. Statement (B) is incorrect. Statement (C): The probability density | ψ | 2 at the nucleus ( r = 0 ) is non-zero only for s orbitals ( l = 0 ). For p orbitals ( l = 1 ), the wave function is zero at the nucleus, so | ψ | 2 = 0 . Statement (C) is correct. Statement (D): The orbital angular momentum is given by l ( l + 1 ) h 2 π . For a d orbital, l = 2 , so the angular momentum is 2 ( 2 + 1 ) h 2 π = 6 h 2 π . Statement (D) is correct. Therefore, statements (C) and (D) are correct. Answer: (C), (D)

Statement (B): For a single-electron system like He+, the energy of an electron depends only on the principal quantum number n. Thus, the 3s, 3p, and 3d orbitals are degenerate, meaning their energies are equal. Statement (B) is incorrect.

Statement (C): The probability density |ψ|2 at the nucleus (r=0) is non-zero only for s orbitals (l=0). For p orbitals (l=1), the wave function is zero at the nucleus, so |ψ|2=0. Statement (C) is correct.

Statement (D): The orbital angular momentum is given by l(l+1)h2π. For a d orbital, l=2, so the angular momentum is 2(2+1)h2π=6h2π. Statement (D) is correct.

Therefore, statements (C) and (D) are correct.

Answer: (C), (D)

questions.solutionText: Solution Positional isomers are compounds that have the same molecular formula, the same carbon skeleton, and the same functional group, but differ in the position of the functional group on the carbon chain. Let us analyze the given options: In option (1), the compounds are CH 3 − CH 2 − CH 2 − C ( = O ) − CH 3 (Pentan-2-one) and CH 3 − CH 2 − C ( = O ) − CH 2 − CH 3 (Pentan-3-one). Both have a straight chain of five carbon atoms and a ketone functional group. They differ only in the position of the carbonyl group (at C-2 and C-3, respectively). Therefore, they are positional isomers. In option (2), the compounds are Pentan-2-one (a ketone) and 3-Methylbutanal (an aldehyde). Since they have different functional groups, they are functional isomers. In option (3), the compounds are Pentan-3-one (a ketone) and 3-Methylbutanal (an aldehyde). They are functional isomers. In option (4), the compounds are Pentanal (an aldehyde) and Pentan-2-one (a ketone). They are also functional isomers. Answer:

questions.text: A thermodynamic system undergoes a three-step cyclic process A → B → C → A . - Process A → B: The system absorbs 5 0 k J of heat and performs 2 0 k J of work on its surroundings. - Process B → C: The system undergoes an isochoric (constant volume) process, during which its internal energy decreases by 4 0 k J . - Process C → A: 3 0 k J of work is done on the system to return it to its initial state A. What is the heat exchanged during the process C → A ?

questions.html:
A thermodynamic system undergoes a three-step cyclic process A→B→C→A.
- Process A → B: The system absorbs 50 kJ of heat and performs 20 kJ of work on its surroundings.
- Process B → C: The system undergoes an isochoric (constant volume) process, during which its internal energy decreases by 40 kJ.
- Process C → A: 30 kJ of work is done on the system to return it to its initial state A.

What is the heat exchanged during the process C→A ?
questions.questionHtml:
A thermodynamic system undergoes a three-step cyclic process A→B→C→A.
- Process A → B: The system absorbs 50 kJ of heat and performs 20 kJ of work on its surroundings.
- Process B → C: The system undergoes an isochoric (constant volume) process, during which its internal energy decreases by 40 kJ.
- Process C → A: 30 kJ of work is done on the system to return it to its initial state A.

What is the heat exchanged during the process C→A ?
questions.solutionText: Solution Using the first law of thermodynamics, Q = Δ U + W , where Q is heat absorbed and W is work done by the system. For the process A → B : Q A B = 50 kJ W A B = 20 kJ Δ U A B = Q A B − W A B = 50 − 20 = 30 kJ For the process B → C : The process is isochoric, so W B C = 0 . The internal energy decreases by 40 kJ , so Δ U B C = − 40 kJ . For the process C → A : Work is done on the system, so W C A = − 30 kJ . For a cyclic process, the net change in internal energy is zero: Δ U A B + Δ U B C + Δ U C A = 0 30 − 40 + Δ U C A = 0 ⇒ Δ U C A = 10 kJ The heat exchanged during C → A is: Q C A = Δ U C A + W C A = 10 + ( − 30 ) = − 20 kJ The negative sign indicates that 20 kJ of heat is released. Answer: 20 kJ released
questions.solution: Solution Using the first law of thermodynamics, Q = Δ U + W , where Q is heat absorbed and W is work done by the system. For the process A → B : Q A B = 50 kJ W A B = 20 kJ Δ U A B = Q A B − W A B = 50 − 20 = 30 kJ For the process B → C : The process is isochoric, so W B C = 0 . The internal energy decreases by 40 kJ , so Δ U B C = − 40 kJ . For the process C → A : Work is done on the system, so W C A = − 30 kJ . For a cyclic process, the net change in internal energy is zero: Δ U A B + Δ U B C + Δ U C A = 0 30 − 40 + Δ U C A = 0 ⇒ Δ U C A = 10 kJ The heat exchanged during C → A is: Q C A = Δ U C A + W C A = 10 + ( − 30 ) = − 20 kJ The negative sign indicates that 20 kJ of heat is released. Answer: 20 kJ released
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Using the first law of thermodynamics, Q=ΔU+W, where Q is heat absorbed and W is work done by the system.

For the process A→B:
QAB=50 kJ
WAB=20 kJ
ΔUAB=QAB−WAB=50−20=30 kJ

For the process B→C:
The process is isochoric, so WBC=0.
The internal energy decreases by 40 kJ, so ΔUBC=−40 kJ.

For the process C→A:
Work is done on the system, so WCA=−30 kJ.
For a cyclic process, the net change in internal energy is zero:
ΔUAB+ΔUBC+ΔUCA=0
30−40+ΔUCA=0⇒ΔUCA=10 kJ

The heat exchanged during C→A is:
QCA=ΔUCA+WCA=10+(−30)=−20 kJ

The negative sign indicates that 20 kJ of heat is released.

Answer: 20 kJ released

questions.prompt: The major product of the following reaction is, → ( 2 ) H 2 O ( 1 ) AlH ( i − Bu ) 2 , 200 K
questions.html:
The major product of the following reaction is,

→(2)H2O(1)AlH(i−Bu)2,200 K
questions.questionHtml:
The major product of the following reaction is,

→(2)H2O(1)AlH(i−Bu)2,200 K
questions.solutionText: Solution The given reactant is 3-phenylprop-2-enenitrile (cinnamonitrile), which contains a carbon-carbon double bond ( C=C ) and a nitrile group ( -CN ). The reagent AlH(i-Bu) 2 is Diisobutylaluminium hydride (DIBAL-H). At low temperatures (such as 200 K ), DIBAL-H acts as a selective reducing agent. It reduces nitriles ( -CN ) to imines, which upon subsequent hydrolysis with H 2 O yield aldehydes ( -CHO ). DIBAL-H does not reduce isolated or conjugated carbon-carbon double bonds under these conditions. Therefore, the C=C double bond in the reactant remains intact. The reaction proceeds as follows: Ph-CH=CH-CN → DIBAL-H, 200 K Ph-CH=CH-CH=N-Al(i-Bu) 2 → H 2 O Ph-CH=CH-CHO The major product formed is cinnamaldehyde (3-phenylprop-2-enal), which corresponds to the structure in option (1). Answer:

It reduces nitriles ( -CN ) to imines, which upon subsequent hydrolysis with H 2 O yield aldehydes ( -CHO ). DIBAL-H does not reduce isolated or conjugated carbon-carbon double bonds under these conditions. Therefore, the C=C double bond in the reactant remains intact. The reaction proceeds as follows: Ph-CH=CH-CN → DIBAL-H, 200 K Ph-CH=CH-CH=N-Al(i-Bu) 2 → H 2 O Ph-CH=CH-CHO The major product formed is cinnamaldehyde (3-phenylprop-2-enal), which corresponds to the structure in option (1). Answer:
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
The given reactant is 3-phenylprop-2-enenitrile (cinnamonitrile), which contains a carbon-carbon double bond (C=C) and a nitrile group (-CN).

The reagent AlH(i-Bu)2 is Diisobutylaluminium hydride (DIBAL-H). At low temperatures (such as 200 K), DIBAL-H acts as a selective reducing agent. It reduces nitriles (-CN) to imines, which upon subsequent hydrolysis with H2O yield aldehydes (-CHO).

DIBAL-H does not reduce isolated or conjugated carbon-carbon double bonds under these conditions. Therefore, the C=C double bond in the reactant remains intact.

The reaction proceeds as follows:
Ph-CH=CH-CN→DIBAL-H, 200 KPh-CH=CH-CH=N-Al(i-Bu)2→H2OPh-CH=CH-CHO

The major product formed is cinnamaldehyde (3-phenylprop-2-enal), which corresponds to the structure in option (1).

Answer:

questions.subject: English & Logical Reasoning
questions.text: Passage: DNA degrades quickly after an animal dies, so researchers once believed it impossible to find ancient genetic material. The search for primeval vestiges of DNA took off in the late 1980s after the development of a technique called polymerase chain reaction (PCR), which copies minute quantities of DNA. Armed with PCR, scientists could look for tiny fragments of DNA that might have weathered the millennia unharmed. In recent years, researchers have isolated DNA from 20-million-year-old magnolia leaves and extracted DNA from a 135-million-year-old weevil found in amber. Recently, a team extracted DNA from bone dating back millions of years for the first time. In the frenzied hunt for ancient DNA, microbiologist Scott R. Woodward may have bagged the biggest quarry. Drawing on lessons learned while growing up among the fossil-rich rocks of eastern Utah, Woodward and his team became the first people to find genetic material belonging to a dinosaur. Woodward, whose grandfather was a coal miner, knew that mines in the area often contained dinosaur traces. After six months of looking, Woodward pulled two bone fragments from a Cretaceous siltstone layer directly atop a coal seam. Impeded by an unstable mine roof, Woodward's team could not recover any more bone samples. The siltstone apparently inhibited fossilization and preserved much of the original cell structure in the bone. Researchers isolated strands of DNA from both fragments and used PCR to copy a segment that codes for a protein called cytochrome b. Once they had made many copies, they could determine the DNA sequence. Throughout their work, the biologists took precautions to avoid contaminating the samples with modern DNA or ancient material found within the coal. According to Woodward, circumstantial evidence indicates that the bone fragments belong to one or two species of dinosaurs. Dinosaur tracks are abundant in this coal formation, and the bones visible in the mine were larger than those of a crocodile—the biggest non-dinosaur known in these rocks. Woodward explains variation found in the DNA as a result of damage to the ancient DNA, which caused the PCR technique to alter the original sequence. Scientists had hoped to use the DNA to resolve debate about the relationship among birds, dinosaurs, and other reptiles. But the cytochrome b fragments were too short to offer meaningful phylogenetic information, says Woodward. Utah's state paleontologist believes that the fragments found by Woodward could definitely be dinosaur in origin. Other researchers, however, question the identity of the DNA strands. Because the copies of the cytochrome b sequence varied considerably, they wonder whether the DNA comes from several organisms. Question According to information put forth and argued by the author in the passage cytochrome b was useful to the scientists because:

questions.html:
Passage:
DNA degrades quickly after an animal dies, so researchers once believed it impossible to find ancient genetic material. The search for primeval vestiges of DNA took off in the late 1980s after the development of a technique called polymerase chain reaction (PCR), which copies minute quantities of DNA. Armed with PCR, scientists could look for tiny fragments of DNA that might have weathered the millennia unharmed. In recent years, researchers have isolated DNA from 20-million-year-old magnolia leaves and extracted DNA from a 135-million-year-old weevil found in amber. Recently, a team extracted DNA from bone dating back millions of years for the first time. In the frenzied hunt for ancient DNA, microbiologist Scott R. Woodward may have bagged the biggest quarry. Drawing on lessons learned while growing up among the fossil-rich rocks of eastern Utah, Woodward and his team became the first people to find genetic material belonging to a dinosaur. Woodward, whose grandfather was a coal miner, knew that mines in the area often contained dinosaur traces. After six months of looking, Woodward pulled two bone fragments from a Cretaceous siltstone layer directly atop a coal seam. Impeded by an unstable mine roof, Woodward's team could not recover any more bone samples. The siltstone apparently inhibited fossilization and preserved much of the original cell structure in the bone.

Researchers isolated strands of DNA from both fragments and used PCR to copy a segment that codes for a protein called cytochrome b. Once they had made many copies, they could determine the DNA sequence. Throughout their work, the biologists took precautions to avoid contaminating the samples with modern DNA or ancient material found within the coal. According to Woodward, circumstantial evidence indicates that the bone fragments belong to one or two species of dinosaurs. Dinosaur tracks are abundant in this coal formation, and the bones visible in the mine were larger than those of a crocodile—the biggest non-dinosaur known in these rocks. Woodward explains variation found in the DNA as a result of damage to the ancient DNA, which caused the PCR technique to alter the original sequence. Scientists had hoped to use the DNA to resolve debate about the relationship among birds, dinosaurs, and other reptiles. But the cytochrome b fragments were too short to offer meaningful phylogenetic information, says Woodward. Utah's state paleontologist believes that the fragments found by Woodward could definitely be dinosaur in origin. Other researchers, however, question the identity of the DNA strands. Because the copies of the cytochrome b sequence varied considerably, they wonder whether the DNA comes from several organisms.

Question

Passage:
DNA degrades quickly after an animal dies, so researchers once believed it impossible to find ancient genetic material. Armed with PCR, scientists could look for tiny fragments of DNA that might have weathered the millennia unharmed. In recent years, researchers have isolated DNA from 20-million-year-old magnolia leaves and extracted DNA from a 135-million-year-old weevil found in amber. Recently, a team extracted DNA from bone dating back millions of years for the first time. In the frenzied hunt for ancient DNA, microbiologist Scott R. Woodward may have bagged the biggest quarry. Drawing on lessons learned while growing up among the fossil-rich rocks of eastern Utah, Woodward and his team became the first people to find genetic material belonging to a dinosaur. Woodward, whose grandfather was a coal miner, knew that mines in the area often contained dinosaur traces. After six months of looking, Woodward pulled two bone fragments from a Cretaceous siltstone layer directly atop a coal seam. Impeded by an unstable mine roof, Woodward's team could not recover any more bone samples. The siltstone apparently inhibited fossilization and preserved much of the original cell structure in the bone.

Researchers isolated strands of DNA from both fragments and used PCR to copy a segment that codes for a protein called cytochrome b. Once they had made many copies, they could determine the DNA sequence. Throughout their work, the biologists took precautions to avoid contaminating the samples with modern DNA or ancient material found within the coal. According to Woodward, circumstantial evidence indicates that the bone fragments belong to one or two species of dinosaurs. Dinosaur tracks are abundant in this coal formation, and the bones visible in the mine were larger than those of a crocodile—the biggest non-dinosaur known in these rocks. Woodward explains variation found in the DNA as a result of damage to the ancient DNA, which caused the PCR technique to alter the original sequence. Scientists had hoped to use the DNA to resolve debate about the relationship among birds, dinosaurs, and other reptiles. But the cytochrome b fragments were too short to offer meaningful phylogenetic information, says Woodward. Utah's state paleontologist believes that the fragments found by Woodward could definitely be dinosaur in origin. Other researchers, however, question the identity of the DNA strands. Because the copies of the cytochrome b sequence varied considerably, they wonder whether the DNA comes from several organisms.

Question
According to information put forth and argued by the author in the passage cytochrome b was useful to the scientists because:
questions.solutionText: Solution Based on the passage, the researchers isolated strands of DNA and used PCR to copy a segment that codes for cytochrome b. Once many copies were made, they could determine the DNA sequence. This indicates that the segment coding for cytochrome b provided a DNA template that could be copied and studied. Option (2) is incorrect because the passage explicitly states that the cytochrome b fragments were too short to offer meaningful phylogenetic information. Option (3) is incorrect because the passage mentions that circumstantial evidence indicates the fragments belong to one or two species, and other researchers question the identity of the DNA strands. Option (4) is incorrect because the realization that the bones belonged to a dinosaur rather than a crocodile was based on the physical size of the bones visible in the mine, not on the cytochrome b. Answer: it provided the scientists with a DNA template that could be studied.

Directions: In the question given below, a sentence with a blank is given. Choose the alternative that completes the sentence grammatically and contextually.

I take profound solace in the conviction that, despite the myriad demands on my time, I have not been ________ in discharging the duties requisite for their moral edification.
questions.solutionText: Solution The sentence conveys that the speaker finds comfort in the fact that they have fulfilled their duties well, despite having many demands on their time. Therefore, the phrase "have not been ________" must mean "have not been negligent or careless". Let us evaluate the given options: "Scrupulous" means diligent, thorough, and extremely attentive to details. "Assiduous" means showing great care and perseverance. "Remiss" means lacking care or attention to duty; negligent. "Overweening" means showing excessive confidence or pride. Using "remiss" fits perfectly, as "not been remiss" means not being negligent in discharging duties. Answer: remiss

questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
The sentence conveys that the speaker finds comfort in the fact that they have fulfilled their duties well, despite having many demands on their time. Therefore, the phrase "have not been ________" must mean "have not been negligent or careless".

Let us evaluate the given options:
"Scrupulous" means diligent, thorough, and extremely attentive to details.
"Assiduous" means showing great care and perseverance.
"Remiss" means lacking care or attention to duty; negligent.
"Overweening" means showing excessive confidence or pride.

Using "remiss" fits perfectly, as "not been remiss" means not being negligent in discharging duties.

Answer: remiss

questions.subject: English & Logical Reasoning
questions.text: Paragraph: Before the widespread adoption of the telegraph in the mid-19th century, timekeeping was a strictly local affair. Each town set its town square clock to noon when the sun reached its highest point in the sky locally. This meant that when it was exactly 12:00 PM in Washington, D.C., it was approximately 12:12 PM in New York City and 11:43 AM in Pittsburgh. This localized, sun-based system was perfectly manageable when the fastest mode of human transportation was a horse-drawn carriage. However, the rapid expansion of the railroad networks created unprecedented logistical nightmares. Conductors struggled to coordinate complex schedules across dozens of shifting, localized time zones, frequently leading to missed connections and, tragically, head-on train collisions on single-track lines. The telegraph provided the crucial solution by allowing instantaneous communication across vast distances. Astronomical observatories began using telegraph lines to transmit a synchronized, electrical "time signal" to railroad stations simultaneously, ultimately paving the way for the adoption of standardized, regional time zones. Question: what was the primary reason the localized timekeeping system became untenable?

questions.solutionText: Solution The passage explicitly states that the localized timekeeping system was manageable during the era of horse-drawn carriages but became an "unprecedented logistical nightmare" with the "rapid expansion of the railroad networks." The complexity of coordinating schedules across many local time zones led to missed connections and train collisions, making the old system untenable and necessitating a unified time system. Answer: The speed and complexity of the growing railroad networks demanded a unified schedule to prevent logistical failures and accidents.

questions.subject: English & Logical Reasoning
questions.text: Paragraph: Every October, the town of Glenhill transforms into a hub of culinary excitement for the Annual Autumn Bake-Off. "The Sugar Mill" naturally serves as the unofficial headquarters for the festivities, decorated top-to-bottom in orange bunting and dried cornstalks. While Mrs. Thompson has traditionally served as the head judge for the competition, she decided to step down from the panel this year. Instead, she chose to dedicate her time to mentoring her granddaughter, Lily, who is entering the contest for the very first time. Lily has spent the past three weeks meticulously perfecting her spiced apple and cranberry tart, hoping to impress the new judges and take home the highly coveted Golden Whisk trophy. For the residents of Glenhill, however, the true prize of the Bake-Off isn't the trophy, but the deep sense of unity the event brings to their small community. Question: According to the passage, why is Mrs. Thompson not acting as the head judge for this year's Autumn Bake-Off?

questions.solutionText: Solution The subject of the underlined clause is "the number", which is a singular noun and must take a singular verb. Therefore, "was" is the correct verb form. The phrase "a number of" takes a plural verb, but "the number of" takes a singular verb. Furthermore, "whether" is preferred over "if" when introducing a noun clause that represents an alternative or a subject of discussion. Options using "were" are grammatically incorrect because they do not agree with the singular subject "the number". Option (3) incorrectly implies that the students themselves would decline, rather than the number of students. Thus, the original phrasing is grammatically correct and conveys the intended meaning perfectly. Answer: whether the number of students studying European languages was likely

Despite cultural narratives that sometimes romanticize the rugged individualist, humanity is fundamentally a ________ species, instinctively seeking out social cohesion and communal interaction.
questions.solutionText: Solution The sentence contrasts the idea of a "rugged individualist" with humanity's true nature, which is described as "instinctively seeking out social cohesion and communal interaction." The word "gregarious" means fond of company or sociable, which perfectly aligns with the description of seeking social cohesion. "Solipsistic" refers to extreme egocentrism, "misanthropic" means disliking humankind, and "taciturn" means reserved in speech. None of these fit the context of communal interaction. Answer: gregarious

Answer: gregarious

Answer: gregarious

questions.subject: English & Logical Reasoning
questions.text: Passage: The Rainforest Alliance, an arbiter of ethical agricultural practices, recently revoked the coveted "green frog seal" from a consortium of Assam Company tea plantations. Company representatives attributed this decertification to an isolated "minor oversight" at the Hajua estate, where laborers were permitted to spray a botanical extract without personal protective equipment (PPE) under the mistaken belief that the substance was entirely benign. However, owing to the Rainforest Alliance's stringent "single cluster" regulatory framework, this localized infraction precipitated the automatic and immediate decertification of the company's entire network of interconnected estates. Question: Which of the following statements best encapsulates the main idea of the passage?

Company representatives attributed this decertification to an isolated "minor oversight" at the Hajua estate, where laborers were permitted to spray a botanical extract without personal protective equipment (PPE) under the mistaken belief that the substance was entirely benign. However, owing to the Rainforest Alliance's stringent "single cluster" regulatory framework, this localized infraction precipitated the automatic and immediate decertification of the company's entire network of interconnected estates. Question: Which of the following statements best encapsulates the main idea of the passage?

questions.solutionText: Solution The passage focuses on a specific event where the Assam Company lost its Rainforest Alliance certification across all its estates because of a single safety violation at one estate, due to the "single cluster" rule. Option 4 accurately captures this cause-and-effect relationship, highlighting the localized failure, the systemic loss of certification, and the interconnected regulatory standards that caused it. Option 1 is too broad and generalizes the situation to all agricultural conglomerates. Option 2 introduces concepts like environmental sustainability which are not the primary focus of the passage. Option 3 mischaracterizes the substance as a "hazardous chemical spray" and focuses on "safety training" rather than the lack of PPE and the systemic regulatory framework. Answer: A localized failure to enforce safety protocols on a single estate triggered a systemic loss of ethical certification for the Assam Company due to interconnected regulatory standards.

questions.solutionText: Solution Sentence A introduces the main subject, Artificial General Intelligence (AGI), and its core characteristic of cognitive adaptability. Sentence B further defines AGI by contrasting it with narrow AI and stating its ultimate aim: to possess human-like understanding. Sentence E directly connects to Sentence B by referring to "This ambitious goal" (the aim to possess human-like understanding) and elaborates on what it entails. Sentence C discusses the philosophical implications of potentially realizing "such a technology" (AGI as described in the preceding sentences). Sentence D provides a concluding contrast with "However," presenting the skeptical viewpoint that achieving this realization is fundamentally impossible. Thus, the correct and logical order is A - B - E - C - D. Answer: A - B - E - C - D

questions.solutionText: Solution A complex sentence contains one independent clause and at least one dependent clause. The original sentence is a compound sentence joined by the coordinating conjunction 'but'. Option (1) uses a prepositional phrase ('Despite conducting...'), making it a simple sentence. Option (2) uses a conjunctive adverb ('nevertheless') to join two independent clauses, keeping it a compound sentence. Option (3) uses the subordinating conjunction 'Even though' to introduce a dependent clause ('Even though the researchers conducted exhaustive clinical trials'), followed by an independent clause. This correctly forms a complex sentence while preserving the original meaning. Option (4) uses a participial phrase and alters the original meaning. Answer: Even though the researchers conducted exhaustive clinical trials, they ultimately failed to isolate the elusive pathogen.

Having meticulously analyzed the archaic manuscripts for over a decade, the true origins of the lost civilization finally became apparent to the eccentric historian.
questions.solutionText: Solution The sentence contains a dangling modifier. The participial phrase "Having meticulously analyzed the archaic manuscripts for over a decade" describes an action performed by a person. However, the subject of the main clause is "the true origins of the lost civilization", which illogically implies that the "origins" analyzed the manuscripts. To correct this, the subject of the main clause must be the person who performed the action, such as "the eccentric historian". Therefore, the error lies in the third part of the sentence. Answer: the true origins of the lost civilization

questions.subject: English & Logical Reasoning
questions.text: At the reading of his will, the eccentric billionaire Arthur Blackwood—who was famously an only child—left his entire vast fortune to one mysterious woman. The single line in his will read: "All my worldly possessions shall belong to the mother of my father's only granddaughter." Based on this statement, who inherits the Blackwood estate?

questions.questionHtml:
At the reading of his will, the eccentric billionaire Arthur Blackwood—who was famously an only child—left his entire vast fortune to one mysterious woman. The single line in his will read:

"All my worldly possessions shall belong to the mother of my father's only granddaughter."

Based on this statement, who inherits the Blackwood estate?
questions.solutionText: Solution Arthur is an only child, which means his father has no other children. Therefore, Arthur's father's granddaughter must be Arthur's own daughter. The phrase "my father's only granddaughter" refers to Arthur's daughter. The will leaves the fortune to the "mother of" this granddaughter. The mother of Arthur's daughter is Arthur's wife. Answer: Arthur's wife

questions.subject: English & Logical Reasoning
questions.text: Eight players P, Q, R, S, T, U, V, and W participated in a badminton tournament. In the first round, these eight players were divided into two groups of four. In each group, each player played twice against each of the other three players. Every match had a decisive result (no ties). The players with the highest and second-highest number of wins in their respective groups reached the semifinal. Q, U, W, and V were the four players who qualified. It is also known that in the first round: - Each player within a group had a different number of wins. - Q was undefeated in the first round. - R lost both of his matches against Q and both against W , but won exactly one match against S. - V won both of his matches against U . - P and W won the same number of matches. Who won the least number of matches after the first round?

In each group, each player played twice against each of the other three players. Every match had a decisive result (no ties). The players with the highest and second-highest number of wins in their respective groups reached the semifinal. Q, U, W, and V were the four players who qualified.

It is also known that in the first round:
- Each player within a group had a different number of wins.
- Q was undefeated in the first round.
- R lost both of his matches against Q and both against W , but won exactly one match against S.
- V won both of his matches against U .

questions.questionHtml:
Eight players P, Q, R, S, T, U, V, and W participated in a badminton tournament. In the first round, these eight players were divided into two groups of four. In each group, each player played twice against each of the other three players. Every match had a decisive result (no ties). The players with the highest and second-highest number of wins in their respective groups reached the semifinal. Q, U, W, and V were the four players who qualified.

It is also known that in the first round:
- Each player within a group had a different number of wins.
- Q was undefeated in the first round.
- R lost both of his matches against Q and both against W , but won exactly one match against S.
- V won both of his matches against U .
- P and W won the same number of matches.

Who won the least number of matches after the first round?
questions.solutionText: Solution Total number of matches in each group is 4 C 2 × 2 = 12 . Thus, the total number of wins in each group is 12 . Since Q was undefeated, Q won all 6 of his matches. R played against Q, W, and S. This implies that Group 1 consists of Q, R, W, and S. Consequently, Group 2 consists of P, T, U, and V. In Group 1, Q won 6 matches. R lost 2 matches to Q, 2 matches to W, and won exactly 1 match against S. Therefore, R won exactly 1 match. The qualifiers from Group 1 are Q and W, meaning W had the second-highest wins in the group. Let the number of wins for W and S be w and s respectively. The sum of wins in Group 1 is: 6 + w + s + 1 = 12 ⇒ w + s = 5 Since all players in a group have a distinct number of wins and w > s , the possible pairs for ( w , s ) are ( 4 , 1 ) and ( 3 , 2 ) . Since R already has 1 win, s cannot be 1 . Thus, w = 3 and s = 2 . In Group 2, the qualifiers are U and V, meaning they have the highest and second-highest wins. We are given that P and W won the same number of matches, so P won 3 matches. Let the wins of U, V, and T be u , v , and t respectively. The sum of wins in Group 2 is: u + v + t + 3 = 12 ⇒ u + v + t = 9 Since U and V qualified, their wins must be strictly greater than P's wins, so u > 3 and v > 3 . The only distinct integers greater than 3 that can sum to ≤ 9 are 4 and 5 . Thus, the wins of U and V are 4 and 5 in some order. This implies t = 9 − ( 4 + 5 ) = 0 . Therefore, T won 0 matches, which is the least number of matches won by any player after the first round. Answer: T

The qualifiers from Group 1 are Q and W, meaning W had the second-highest wins in the group. Let the number of wins for W and S be w and s respectively. The sum of wins in Group 1 is:

6+w+s+1=12⇒w+s=5

Since all players in a group have a distinct number of wins and w>s, the possible pairs for (w,s) are (4,1) and (3,2). Since R already has 1 win, s cannot be 1. Thus, w=3 and s=2.

In Group 2, the qualifiers are U and V, meaning they have the highest and second-highest wins. We are given that P and W won the same number of matches, so P won 3 matches.

Let the wins of U, V, and T be u,v, and t respectively. The sum of wins in Group 2 is:

u+v+t+3=12⇒u+v+t=9

Since U and V qualified, their wins must be strictly greater than P's wins, so u>3 and v>3. The only distinct integers greater than 3 that can sum to ≤9 are 4 and 5.

Thus, the wins of U and V are 4 and 5 in some order. This implies t=9−(4+5)=0.

Therefore, T won 0 matches, which is the least number of matches won by any player after the first round.

Answer: T

Directions: Read the passage carefully and choose the best alternative.

Passage:
Despite a massive global surge in demand for raw lithium over the past five years, the total volume of raw lithium exported by Country X—historically one of the world's leading suppliers—has sharply declined during this period.

Question:
Each of the following, if true, could help to explain the decline in Country X’s raw lithium exports EXCEPT:
questions.solutionText: Solution The passage states that Country X's raw lithium exports have sharply declined despite a global surge in demand. We need to find the option that does NOT explain this decline. Option (1) explains the decline because steep export tariffs on unrefined minerals would discourage the export of raw lithium. Option (2) explains the decline because domestic battery production facilities are absorbing the locally mined lithium, leaving less available for export. Option (3) states that the currency depreciation makes Country X's raw exports exceptionally inexpensive for foreign buyers. This would typically increase foreign demand and boost exports, not cause a decline. Therefore, this option does not explain the decline. Option (4) explains the decline because labor shortages and infrastructure failures at ports would physically restrict the country's ability to export raw materials. Thus, Option (3) is the only one that fails to explain the decline. Answer: The currency of Country X has significantly depreciated relative to global currencies, making its raw exports exceptionally inexpensive for foreign buyers.

questions.text: K is a place which is located 2 km away in the north-west direction from the capital P . R is another place that is located 2 km away in the south-west direction from K . M is another place and that is located 2 km away in the north-west direction from R . T is yet another place that is located 2 km away in the south-west direction from M . In which direction is T located in relation to P ?

questions.questionHtml: K is a place which is located 2 km away in the north-west direction from the capital P. R is another place that is located 2 km away in the south-west direction from K. M is another place and that is located 2 km away in the north-west direction from R. T is yet another place that is located 2 km away in the south-west direction from M. In which direction is T located in relation to P?
questions.solutionText: Solution Let the capital P be at the origin ( 0 , 0 ) . Let the East direction be along the positive x-axis and North be along the positive y-axis. The displacement vectors for each movement of 2 km are: North-West (NW) direction corresponds to a vector v → N W = − 2 cos ⁡ ( 45 ∘ ) i ^ + 2 sin ⁡ ( 45 ∘ ) j ^ = − 2 i ^ + 2 j ^ . South-West (SW) direction corresponds to a vector v → S W = − 2 cos ⁡ ( 45 ∘ ) i ^ − 2 sin ⁡ ( 45 ∘ ) j ^ = − 2 i ^ − 2 j ^ . The position of K with respect to P is P K → = v → N W = − 2 i ^ + 2 j ^ . The position of R with respect to K is K R → = v → S W = − 2 i ^ − 2 j ^ . The position of M with respect to R is R M → = v → N W = − 2 i ^ + 2 j ^ . The position of T with respect to M is M T → = v → S W = − 2 i ^ − 2 j ^ . The position of T with respect to P is the vector sum of all displacements: P T → = P K → + K R → + R M → + M T → P T → = ( − 2 i ^ + 2 j ^ ) + ( − 2 i ^ − 2 j ^ ) + ( − 2 i ^ + 2 j ^ ) + ( − 2 i ^ − 2 j ^ ) P T → = − 4 2 i ^ + 0 j ^ Since the final position vector has only a negative x-component and zero y-component, T is located exactly to the West of P . Answer: West
questions.solution: Solution Let the capital P be at the origin ( 0 , 0 ) . Let the East direction be along the positive x-axis and North be along the positive y-axis. The displacement vectors for each movement of 2 km are: North-West (NW) direction corresponds to a vector v → N W = − 2 cos ⁡ ( 45 ∘ ) i ^ + 2 sin ⁡ ( 45 ∘ ) j ^ = − 2 i ^ + 2 j ^ . South-West (SW) direction corresponds to a vector v → S W = − 2 cos ⁡ ( 45 ∘ ) i ^ − 2 sin ⁡ ( 45 ∘ ) j ^ = − 2 i ^ − 2 j ^ . The position of K with respect to P is P K → = v → N W = − 2 i ^ + 2 j ^ . The position of R with respect to K is K R → = v → S W = − 2 i ^ − 2 j ^ . The position of M with respect to R is R M → = v → N W = − 2 i ^ + 2 j ^ . The position of T with respect to M is M T → = v → S W = − 2 i ^ − 2 j ^ . The position of T with respect to P is the vector sum of all displacements: P T → = P K → + K R → + R M → + M T → P T → = ( − 2 i ^ + 2 j ^ ) + ( − 2 i ^ − 2 j ^ ) + ( − 2 i ^ + 2 j ^ ) + ( − 2 i ^ − 2 j ^ ) P T → = − 4 2 i ^ + 0 j ^ Since the final position vector has only a negative x-component and zero y-component, T is located exactly to the West of P . Answer: West
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Let the capital P be at the origin (0,0).

Let the East direction be along the positive x-axis and North be along the positive y-axis.

The displacement vectors for each movement of 2 km are:
North-West (NW) direction corresponds to a vector v→NW=−2cos⁡(45∘)i^+2sin⁡(45∘)j^=−2i^+2j^.
South-West (SW) direction corresponds to a vector v→SW=−2cos⁡(45∘)i^−2sin⁡(45∘)j^=−2i^−2j^.

The position of K with respect to P is PK→=v→NW=−2i^+2j^.

The position of R with respect to K is KR→=v→SW=−2i^−2j^.

The position of M with respect to R is RM→=v→NW=−2i^+2j^.

The position of T with respect to M is MT→=v→SW=−2i^−2j^.

The position of T with respect to P is the vector sum of all displacements:
PT→=PK→+KR→+RM→+MT→

PT→=(−2i^+2j^)+(−2i^−2j^)+(−2i^+2j^)+(−2i^−2j^)

PT→=−42i^+0j^

Since the final position vector has only a negative x-component and zero y-component, T is located exactly to the West of P.

Answer: West

Directions: Arrange the following words in a meaningful and logical sequence.

1. Act
2. Presidential Assent
3. Drafting
4. Gazette Notification
5. Parliamentary Debate
6. Bill
questions.solutionText: Solution The logical sequence of the law-making process is as follows: First, the proposed law undergoes Drafting (3). Once drafted, it is introduced in the legislature as a Bill (6). The Bill is then subjected to Parliamentary Debate (5). After being passed by both houses, it is sent for Presidential Assent (2). Upon receiving the assent, the Bill becomes an Act (1). Finally, it is published through a Gazette Notification (4) to come into effect. The correct sequence is 3, 6, 5, 2, 1, 4. Answer: 3, 6, 5, 2, 1, 4
questions.solution: Solution The logical sequence of the law-making process is as follows: First, the proposed law undergoes Drafting (3). Once drafted, it is introduced in the legislature as a Bill (6). The Bill is then subjected to Parliamentary Debate (5). After being passed by both houses, it is sent for Presidential Assent (2). Upon receiving the assent, the Bill becomes an Act (1). Finally, it is published through a Gazette Notification (4) to come into effect. The correct sequence is 3, 6, 5, 2, 1, 4. Answer: 3, 6, 5, 2, 1, 4
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
The logical sequence of the law-making process is as follows:

First, the proposed law undergoes Drafting (3).

Once drafted, it is introduced in the legislature as a Bill (6).

The Bill is then subjected to Parliamentary Debate (5).

After being passed by both houses, it is sent for Presidential Assent (2).

Upon receiving the assent, the Bill becomes an Act (1).

Finally, it is published through a Gazette Notification (4) to come into effect.

The correct sequence is 3, 6, 5, 2, 1, 4.

Answer: 3, 6, 5, 2, 1, 4

questions.text: Identify the next number in the series 2 , 7 , 17 , 32 , 52 , 77 , _ _ _ .

questions.prompt: Identify the next number in the series 2 , 7 , 17 , 32 , 52 , 77 , _ _ _ .
questions.html: Identify the next number in the series 2,7,17,32,52,77,___.
questions.questionHtml: Identify the next number in the series 2,7,17,32,52,77,___.
questions.solutionText: Solution The given series is 2 , 7 , 17 , 32 , 52 , 77 , … Calculating the differences between consecutive terms: 7 − 2 = 5 17 − 7 = 10 32 − 17 = 15 52 − 32 = 20 77 − 52 = 25 The differences form an arithmetic progression: 5 , 10 , 15 , 20 , 25 , … The next difference will be 25 + 5 = 30 . Adding this difference to the last term gives the next number in the series: 77 + 30 = 107 Answer: 107
questions.solution: Solution The given series is 2 , 7 , 17 , 32 , 52 , 77 , … Calculating the differences between consecutive terms: 7 − 2 = 5 17 − 7 = 10 32 − 17 = 15 52 − 32 = 20 77 − 52 = 25 The differences form an arithmetic progression: 5 , 10 , 15 , 20 , 25 , … The next difference will be 25 + 5 = 30 . Adding this difference to the last term gives the next number in the series: 77 + 30 = 107 Answer: 107
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
The given series is 2,7,17,32,52,77,…

Calculating the differences between consecutive terms:
7−2=5
17−7=10
32−17=15
52−32=20
77−52=25

The differences form an arithmetic progression: 5,10,15,20,25,…

The next difference will be 25+5=30.

Adding this difference to the last term gives the next number in the series:
77+30=107

Answer: 107

questions.solutionText: Solution The word CALANDER is coded as CLANAEDR. The first and last letters remain in their original positions. The remaining letters are swapped in pairs. C → C AL → LA AN → NA DE → ED R → R Applying the same pattern to CIRCULAR: C → C IR → RI CU → UC LA → AL R → R The coded word is CRIUCALR. Answer: CRIUCALR

questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
The word CALANDER is coded as CLANAEDR.

The first and last letters remain in their original positions. The remaining letters are swapped in pairs.

C → C
AL → LA
AN → NA
DE → ED
R → R

Applying the same pattern to CIRCULAR:

C → C
IR → RI
CU → UC
LA → AL
R → R

The coded word is CRIUCALR.

Answer: CRIUCALR

questions.text: Kavya's birthday is on Tuesday 4 th July. On what day of the week will be Anika's birthday in the same year, if Anika was born of 15 th August?

questions.html: Kavya's birthday is on Tuesday 4th July. On what day of the week will be Anika's birthday in the same year, if Anika was born of 15th August?
questions.questionHtml: Kavya's birthday is on Tuesday 4th July. On what day of the week will be Anika's birthday in the same year, if Anika was born of 15th August?
questions.solutionText: Solution Days remaining in July = 31 − 4 = 27 Days in August = 15 Total number of days = 27 + 15 = 42 Number of odd days = 42 ( mod 7 ) = 0 Since the number of odd days is 0 , the day of the week on 15 th August will be the same as on 4 th July, which is Tuesday. Answer: Tuesday
questions.solution: Solution Days remaining in July = 31 − 4 = 27 Days in August = 15 Total number of days = 27 + 15 = 42 Number of odd days = 42 ( mod 7 ) = 0 Since the number of odd days is 0 , the day of the week on 15 th August will be the same as on 4 th July, which is Tuesday. Answer: Tuesday
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Days remaining in July = 31−4=27

Days in August = 15

Total number of days = 27+15=42

Number of odd days = 42(mod7)=0

Since the number of odd days is 0, the day of the week on 15th August will be the same as on 4th July, which is Tuesday.

Answer: Tuesday

questions.subject: English & Logical Reasoning
questions.text: A wall clock is placed on a table in such a way that at 4:30 PM, the minute hand points exactly towards the East. In which direction will the hour hand point at 10:30 PM?
questions.questionText: A wall clock is placed on a table in such a way that at 4:30 PM, the minute hand points exactly towards the East. In which direction will the hour hand point at 10:30 PM?
questions.question: A wall clock is placed on a table in such a way that at 4:30 PM, the minute hand points exactly towards the East. In which direction will the hour hand point at 10:30 PM?
questions.prompt: A wall clock is placed on a table in such a way that at 4:30 PM, the minute hand points exactly towards the East. In which direction will the hour hand point at 10:30 PM?
questions.html:
A wall clock is placed on a table in such a way that at 4:30 PM, the minute hand points exactly towards the East.

In which direction will the hour hand point at 10:30 PM?
questions.questionHtml:
A wall clock is placed on a table in such a way that at 4:30 PM, the minute hand points exactly towards the East.

In which direction will the hour hand point at 10:30 PM?
questions.solutionText: Solution At 4:30 PM, the minute hand is exactly at the 6 o'clock position. It is given that this position points towards the East. The numbers on a clock face are arranged in a clockwise order. The standard geographic directions in clockwise order are North, East, South, and West. Since the 6 o'clock position corresponds to East, moving 90 degrees clockwise brings us to the 9 o'clock position, which must correspond to South. Moving another 90 degrees clockwise brings us to the 12 o'clock position, which corresponds to West. At 10:30 PM, the hour hand is positioned exactly halfway between 10 and 11. Since the numbers 10 and 11 lie between 9 (South) and 12 (West) on the clock face, the hour hand will point between South and West. Therefore, the hour hand points towards the South-West direction. Answer: South-West

questions.questionHtml: If the positions of the fifth and twelfth letters of the word GLORIFICATIONS are interchanged, and likewise the positions of the fourth and fourteenth letters, the third and tenth letters, the second and eleventh letters and the first and thirteenth letters are interchanged, which of the following will be the twelfth letter from the right end?
questions.solutionText: Solution The word GLORIFICATIONS has 14 letters. Numbering the letters from left to right: 1 -G, 2 -L, 3 -O, 4 -R, 5 -I, 6 -F, 7 -I, 8 -C, 9 -A, 10 -T, 11 -I, 12 -O, 13 -N, 14 -S. The twelfth letter from the right end in a 14 -letter word is the ( 14 − 12 + 1 ) = 3 rd letter from the left end. According to the given condition, the 3 rd letter is interchanged with the 10 th letter. The 10 th letter in the original word is T. Therefore, the 3 rd letter from the left end (which is the 12 th letter from the right end) in the new arrangement will be T. Answer: T

questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
The word GLORIFICATIONS has 14 letters.

Numbering the letters from left to right:
1-G, 2-L, 3-O, 4-R, 5-I, 6-F, 7-I, 8-C, 9-A, 10-T, 11-I, 12-O, 13-N, 14-S.

The twelfth letter from the right end in a 14-letter word is the (14−12+1)=3rd letter from the left end.

According to the given condition, the 3rd letter is interchanged with the 10th letter.

The 10th letter in the original word is T.

Therefore, the 3rd letter from the left end (which is the 12th letter from the right end) in the new arrangement will be T.

Answer: T
